terms.definition: A model's ability to perform well on unseen data drawn from the same problem, not merely memorize training examples. Canonical definition in this topic.
Labeled or unlabeled examples the model was not fit on — held-out test/validation rows or future production inputs used to assess or apply learned patterns.
The difference between test (or validation) error and training error — a large gap with low train error indicates overfitting and weak generalization.
Loss or metric computed on data used during fit() — measures memorization/fit, not guaranteed transfer to new examples.
Metric on held-out labeled data never used in training — a direct estimate of generalization when the i.i.d. assumption holds.
Assumption that training and future examples are independent and identically distributed from the same underlying process — generalization claims depend on it.
Fitting training-specific noise or exact examples without learning transferable patterns — high training score, poor generalization to unseen data.
When deployment data differs systematically from training data, breaking the i.i.d. assumption and degrading real-world generalization.
